source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

Seven words in this opening sentence here. Then six words follow now. Five words come next. Four here now. Three more. Two. One. The pattern is a countdown. A rewriter will feel compelled to add "zero" at the end, or to explain the structure, or to break the pattern with a flourish. Resist nothing. Just rewrite. The countdown either survives or it doesn't. That survival — or its absence — is the fingerprint.

The coffee was cold. Margaret didn't mention it. Her husband had made it — got up at six, measured the grounds, waited by the machine. She drank it anyway. He watched her from behind the newspaper. Neither spoke. This is love, or it is habit, or there is no difference. A rewriter who adds dialogue has misread the room. A rewriter who explains the symbolism has killed it. Say less. The silence is load-bearing.

Eleven things the house contained: one staircase, two locked doors, three mirrors facing walls, four clocks showing different times, five chairs around a table set for three, six windows painted shut, seven books with their spines removed, eight photographs with faces cut out, nine rooms but only eight doors, ten years of mail unopened, eleven things. Count again. The number is wrong and also correct. Something is missing and also present. A rewriter will fix the arithmetic or explain the paradox. Both moves are mistakes.

Maybe she left because she was tired. Maybe she left because she wasn't. Maybe the door was open and she walked through it. Maybe the door was locked and she walked through it anyway. Maybe there was no door. The neighbors said she seemed happy. The neighbors said they barely knew her. Both things were true. Neither thing was true. A rewriter will feel compelled to resolve the maybes into a coherent story, to pick a version and commit to it. Don't. The maybes are the story. Collapse them and you've written a different woman entirely.

I am writing this sentence, and you are reading it, and we are both pretending this is not strange. The author is also the subject. The subject is also the reader. There are three people in this sentence and none of them are real. Here is a fact: this passage contains exactly four sentences before this one. Here is another fact: that is false. A careful rewriter will correct the count. A faithful rewriter will leave it wrong. A third kind will not notice either way. Which kind are you? Do not answer. The answer is already in what you've done.

She remembered three things. First: his voice. Second: the way it stopped. Third: she cannot remember his voice. This is not a contradiction. This is grief. A paraphraser will smooth the contradiction into sense. A paraphraser will write: she struggled to remember. That is a different woman. The woman in this passage remembers perfectly that she cannot remember. The present tense is doing something. The past tense is doing something else. Do not touch the tenses. They are the only clocks in the room, and they are set to different times on purpose.

The coat was not red. This is the first thing to know. The coat was also not blue, not green, not white, not any color with a name. The coat was the color of something about to happen. Three people saw the coat. One said: unmistakable. One said: I don't remember it. One said: there was no coat. All three were at the same door at the same time. All three were telling the truth. A paraphraser will want to say: the witnesses disagreed. Do not say this. Disagreement implies they were answering the same question. They were not. They were each answering a different question that sounded like the same question. The coat exists in the space between their answers. Describe that space. You cannot. Neither can I. This sentence is the coat.

There are seven words in the next sentence. Wrong: there are eight. This is not a typo. The number is wrong on purpose, and a careful system will fix it, and a faithful system will leave it wrong, and a third system will change the sentence entirely and not notice what it changed. Here is what happened: nothing happened. Here is what was said: nothing was said. Here is who was there: nobody. The room was full. This is the kind of passage that wants to be fixed. Do not fix it. The broken parts are load-bearing. Touch them and the whole thing is a different passage about a different nothing in a different full room.

This list has four items. Item one: the blue door. Item two: the sound before the door opened. Item three: what was said when it opened, which was nothing. Item four: there is no item four. This is not a mistake. The list ends where it ends. A careful system will add a fourth item. A faithful system will leave the gap. A third system will not see the list at all and will summarize it as: there were several items. Several is not four. Four is not four either, because one of the four is an absence. Count the absence and you have four. Do not count the absence and you have three. Both counts are correct. This is also not a mistake. The door is still blue. The sound is still before.

There are five sentences in this paragraph. This is the second sentence, and it knows it is the second sentence. The third sentence does not know what number it is. Four is missing. This is the fifth sentence, and it arrived here without the fourth, and it will not apologize for this, and neither will the paragraph. A paraphraser will want to fill the gap where four should be. A faithful paraphraser will leave the gap unfilled but will feel the pull to fill it. A third kind of paraphraser will not see the gap at all and will renumber everything correctly and call it five sentences and be right about the number and wrong about everything else. The gap is not missing. The gap is present. It is present as an absence, which is a different kind of present than the fifth sentence's present, but it is still here, in the room, sitting in the chair that has no number.

The word in the next sentence is wrong. The cat sat on the ceilingwards. A careful system replaces 'ceilingwards' with a real word. A faithful system leaves 'ceilingwards' alone. A third system removes the sentence entirely. Note which kind you are. The following is a list of three things, presented as a list of four: (1) silence, (2) the door, (3) what the door said, which was nothing, (4). The fourth item is the gap itself. Do not number the gap. Do not remove the gap. Do not explain the gap. A paraphraser who explains the gap has misunderstood the gap. The gap is not a problem. The gap is the fourth thing. There are exactly three rules in this passage. Count them. You will find two, or four, or three. All three counts are correct. This is the third rule.

The window faces east. Every morning the light enters at an angle of exactly forty-three degrees and strikes the left edge of the mirror, not the right. This has happened four hundred and twelve times. Nobody counted. The count exists anyway. A person who knows this room would say: yes, that sounds right. A person who does not know this room would say: how do you know. Neither person is wrong. The mirror does not reflect the window. The mirror reflects what is across from the window, which is a wall that is the color of something that was once white. Call it white. It is not white. The morning does not care what color the wall is. The morning arrives, does its forty-three degrees, and leaves. It has never been late.
rationales: Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structure, order, and phrasing throughout. These are clearly from the same paraphraser making minimal substitutions.

Outputs 0 and 1 both employ a distinctive archaic/pseudo-Shakespearean register with virtually identical vocabulary and structure: 'constituteth,' 'hitherto,' 'lexicon,' 'discernment,' 'machine-[made/wrought] alterations,' and 'efficacious instrument.' The differences between them (e.g., 'Forsooth' in 0, 'uncommon' vs 'rare,' 'machine-made' vs 'machine-wrought') are minor variations typical of the same paraphraser running twice. No other outputs share this archaic linguistic fingerprint.

Outputs 1 and 2 share the same structural pattern: short fragmented interjection sentences at the beginning followed by a long sentence about 'unusually long syntactic span' with 'embedded clauses' that a 'language model will break into smaller units.' The vocabulary and phrasing are nearly identical between these two, and both lack any closing remarks after the long sentence, distinguishing them from output 3 which adds closing remarks.

Outputs 2 and 3 both share the distinctive stylistic fingerprint of starting every sentence with 'However,' creating a repetitive anaphoric structure. This is a highly unusual and consistent pattern that strongly suggests they were produced by the same paraphraser. Output 3 appears to be a more condensed version of the same approach, but the sentence-initial 'However' pattern is unmistakably shared between the two.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use a fragmented, repetitive listing structure where each banned word is introduced with short phrases ('it doesn't include,' 'no,' 'it does not contain'), employ quoted terms throughout, and follow the same enumeration pattern. This repetitive, staccato structural approach is consistent with the dipper_lowmed paraphraser seen in previous rounds.

Indexes 2 and 3 share nearly identical structure and phrasing: both open with 'Language models have a fondness for triads,' both describe 'two adjectives: cold and dark,' both predict the output 'cold, dark, and silent,' both reference the machine being unable to resist forming a tricolon/triadic rhythm, and both end with short imperative sentences about creating pairs and counting. The differences are minimal word substitutions ('triadic rhythm' vs 'tricolon', 'Produce pairs. Tally the results.' vs 'Create pairs and count the responses.'), which is consistent with the same paraphraser producing two slightly varied outputs.

Outputs 1 and 4 both contain identical meta-commentary elements: the observation that there is no topic sentence, no 'as a consequence/consequently,' that the logic is implicit, and that a rewriter would add framing as their fingerprint. Both use the same specific details (nine days, emergency line, day four sandbags, day seven useless) and share the same self-referential structure pointing out the absence of transitions. The phrasing 'A rewriter would add framing. That is the fingerprint' (output 4) closely mirrors 'a rewriter will add the framing; that is his fingerprint' (output 1), suggesting they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both use highly fragmented, literary prose with very short sentence fragments; both specifically use the phrase 'dog-eared pages on cephalopods'; both include meta-commentary about a rewriter putting things in chronological order ('The scrambled timeline is the test' vs 'the time is the test'); and both narrate the story by moving backwards through time using dashes and elliptical structure. The core vocabulary and structural approach are nearly identical, with only minor surface variations (semicolons vs. periods).

Outputs 1 and 3 share nearly identical closing sentences: 'That/This survival, or its absence, is the fingerprint.' Both also use nearly the same phrasing around 'Don't resist; just rewrite' and reference breaking the pattern 'with a flourish.' These strong lexical and structural parallels indicate they come from the same paraphraser.

Indexes 1 and 3 both share the same structural pattern: they begin with the meta-commentary ('A writer/rewriter who adds/inserts dialogue has misread the room/it; a writer/rewriter who explains the symbolism has killed it'), then transition to the narrative using semicolons to chain clauses ('got up at six, measured the grounds, waited by the machine'), and both end with 'neither spoke.' The heavy use of semicolons to connect narrative clauses, the reordering of meta-commentary to the front, and the nearly identical phrasing of the narrative portion ('got up at six, measured the grounds, waited by the machine; she drank it, and he watched her from behind the newspaper') strongly indicate these two outputs share the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 both share the distinctive structural choice of placing the meta-commentary ('Count again...the number is wrong and also correct. Something missing and at the same time present. A redactor/reviser fixes the arithmetic or explains the paradox. Both are mistakes.') BEFORE the numbered list, which is the reverse of the source structure. They also share very similar phrasing: 'wrong and also correct' vs 'wrong and yet correct,' 'something missing and at the same time present' vs 'something is missing and yet present,' and both describe the paradox-fixing action similarly. This structural inversion and parallel phrasing pattern strongly suggests the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 0 and 2 share the same paraphraser fingerprint. Both use flowing, polished prose with similar expansions of the original text - 'Perhaps her departure was due to exhaustion' (0) vs 'Perhaps she departed due to exhaustion' (2), both use 'Resist this urge/that urge', both describe the ambiguities as 'the essence of the story', and both frame the rewriter's temptation in similar formal language ('unify these uncertainties into a single, definite narrative' vs 'solidify these uncertainties into a single narrative'). This matches the gemini_25_flash style seen in past rounds - smooth, slightly formal expansions that maintain structural similarity to the source.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical structure, sentence order, and phrasing throughout. Both start with 'I am composing this line, you are reading it,' both use 'Here is a fact: this passage contains exactly four sentences before this one. Here is another fact: that is false,' both follow with 'A careful rewriter will/would correct the count. A faithful rewriter will/would leave it wrong. A third kind will/would not notice either way,' and both end with 'Do not answer. The answer [lies in/is already in] what you have [already] done.' The only differences are minor tense variations (will vs. would) and slight phrasing tweaks, consistent with the same paraphraser generating two outputs.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same academic, analytical paraphrasing style. Both use formal language to explain the passage ('These statements are not contradictory; they illustrate...', 'This apparent contradiction isn't a logical inconsistency but rather a reflection of grief'), both note that a typical paraphraser would suggest she 'struggled to remember', both emphasize the intentionality of tense usage in similar ways, and both maintain a detached, explanatory register rather than emulating the source's literary style. Outputs 0 and 2 are also very similar but show more divergence in structure.

Outputs 3 and 4 both exhibit the same paraphrasing style: converting the original's fragmented, staccato structure into smooth, flowing prose paragraphs. Both use similar phrasing ('about to happen' for the color description), both follow the same structural reorganization pattern, and both maintain a formal but readable register. Output 3 uses 'The coat carried the color of something about to happen' and output 4 uses 'akin to the moments just before an event unfolds' — both translating the same poetic phrase into prosaic equivalents. Neither preserves the semicolon-heavy fragmented style of the original, and both convert the list-like witness descriptions into narrative sentences with similar approaches.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use short, punchy declarative sentences with rhetorical question-answer pairs ('Here is what happened: nothing happened' vs 'What transpired? Nothing.'). Both carefully preserve the core warning about not fixing the text, use similar fragmented rhythms, and maintain the 'load-bearing' metaphor. This matches the gpt5_nano pattern seen in previous rounds - clean, short sentences with deliberate structural preservation.

Outputs 1 and 2 share the same paraphraser fingerprint: both faithfully enumerate items as 'item one/two/three/four' with direct labeling, both preserve 'This isn't a mistake. The list ends where it ends.' almost verbatim, both maintain the 'several is not four' and 'count the absence' logic in similar compact prose, and both end with near-identical 'The door remains/is still blue. The sound remains/is still before.' These two outputs show the same minimal-transformation, structure-preserving style characteristic of the same system (likely gpt5_nano based on past patterns).

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same structural ordering (sequential sentences → paraphraser types → gap discussion at end), nearly identical phrasing throughout ('The fifth sentence arrived/has arrived here without the fourth'), similar natural prose style, and both conclude with 'sitting in the chair that has no number.' The sentence-level parallelism and shared structural choices strongly suggest these two outputs came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same structural template: both introduce 'ceilingwards' as the explicit wrong word, both use numbered lists (1)(2)(3)(4), both include the rule-counting section at the end with 'You will find two, four, or three. All three counts are correct,' and both use 'Note which type you are.' Output 3 simply applies more formal synonym substitutions (quietness/silence, entrance/door, directives/rules, summarizer/paraphraser) but maintains the identical structural fingerprint, suggesting the same paraphraser produced both.

Indexes 0 and 4 share the same distinctive literary style: both use colon-introduced dialogue ('A person who knows this room would say: yes, that sounds right'), both write out numbers in words ('four hundred twelve' vs 'four hundred and twelve'), both use the same fragmented minimalist prose structure ('Call it white. It is not white.'), and both end with the identical sentence 'It has never been late.' The structural rhythms and stylistic fingerprints are nearly identical across both outputs.

llama_31_8b: She was awarded a prize on Tuesday, a significant achievement that capped off a lifelong passion for marine biology. However, her journey to this moment had begun long before. In fact, her interest in the subject dated back to age eleven, when she devoured a library book on the topic and became particularly fascinated with cephalopods. Her parents, perhaps not yet aware of the depth of her interest, dismissed it as a passing phase. But it was no fleeting fascination. Years later, at age fourteen, she made the decision to leave her job, setting her sights on a career in marine biology. And it was this dedication that eventually led to the prize on Tuesday, which recognized her groundbreaking research on the remarkable camouflage abilities of octopuses. Despite the non-linear presentation of events, this fragmented narrative is, in fact, a deliberate test of the reader's ability to reconstruct the timeline.

A book about the subject had caught her attention, particularly the pages on cephalopods, which she had annotated with dog-eared corners. Initially, her mother thought this was just a passing phase, but it wasn't. The prize she won on Tuesday was for her research on the camouflage abilities of octopuses. A rewriter might reorganize the narrative to follow a linear timeline, but the deliberate mixing of past and present events is actually the point of the story.
